capstone-domain-model.label: Capstone Domain Model
capstone-domain-model.summary: Build the task objects that carry the tracker domain before adding storage or reports.

try_capstone_domain_model_sketch0.title: Create the base task class
try_capstone_domain_model_sketch0.prompt: Build the first Smart Task Tracker model layer. In `models/base_task.py`, define a `BaseTask` class with `title`, `priority`, and `completed` attributes. New tasks should start with `completed` set to `False`. Add a `mark_done()` method that changes `completed` to `True`. Keep the `summary()` method as an abstract-style base-class contract by raising `NotImplementedError`. In `main.py`, create one `BaseTask` object with title `Draft roadmap` and priority `high`, call `mark_done()`, and print its three attributes on separate lines so you can verify the object state in the output panel.
try_capstone_domain_model_sketch0.hint: This is the first project step. Build a clean starting version, then run it before moving on.

help.concept: The final capstone is progressive. Each step starts from the previous working solution and adds one focused feature.
help.hint_1: This is the first project step. Build a clean starting version, then run it before moving on.
help.hint_2: Use the class names, method names, and file targets from the prompt as your checklist.

main_py.content:
class BaseTask:
    def __init__(self, title, priority):
        self.title = title
        self.priority = priority
        self.completed = False

base = BaseTask("Draft roadmap", "high")
print(base.title)
print(base.priority)
print(base.completed)

capstone_domain_model_step1_bug_task.title: Add the BugTask subclass
capstone_domain_model_step1_bug_task.prompt: Build on the last working Smart Task Tracker snapshot. Keep the existing `BaseTask` class, then add `models/bug_task.py` with a `BugTask` subclass. It should inherit from `BaseTask`, call `super().__init__(title, priority)`, store a `severity` attribute, and override `summary()` to return exactly `BUG: <title> | priority=<priority> | severity=<severity> | done=<completed>`. In `main.py`, create a bug task for `Fix login redirect`, priority `high`, severity `critical`, and print its summary before and after calling `mark_done()`.

tests_check_tasks_py.content: # TODO: add assert-based checks for validation, priority labels, and done counts

main_py.content:
class BugTask:
    def __init__(self, title, priority, severity):
        self.title = title
        self.priority = priority
        self.completed = False
        self.severity = severity

    def mark_done(self):
        self.completed = True

    def summary(self):
        return f"BUG: {self.title} | priority={self.priority} | severity={self.severity} | done={self.completed}"

bug = BugTask("Fix login redirect", "high", "critical")
print(bug.summary())
bug.mark_done()
print(bug.summary())

tests_check_tasks_py.content: # TODO: add assert-based checks for validation, priority labels, and done counts

capstone_domain_model_step2_feature_task.title: Add the FeatureTask subclass with validation
capstone_domain_model_step2_feature_task.prompt: Build on the last working Smart Task Tracker snapshot. Add `models/feature_task.py` with a `FeatureTask` subclass that inherits from `BaseTask`. It should accept `title`, `priority`, and `estimate_points`. Use `super()` for the shared fields. Add validation so `estimate_points` cannot be less than 1; if it is, store `1` instead. Override `summary()` to return exactly `FEATURE: <title> | priority=<priority> | points=<estimate_points> | done=<completed>`. In `main.py`, create one feature task with title `Add dark mode`, priority `medium`, and estimate points `0`, then print its summary. This step matters because the capstone needs encapsulated object rules, not unchecked raw data.

capstone_domain_model_step3_task_service.title: Create a polymorphic task service
capstone_domain_model_step3_task_service.prompt: Build on the full workspace from the last working step. Add `services/task_service.py` with a function named `build_report_lines(tasks)` that returns a list of summary strings for every task in order. The function must work with a mixed list of `BugTask` and `FeatureTask` objects by calling each object's `summary()` method. Do not hard-code separate formatting in the service. In `main.py`, create one bug task (`Fix login redirect`, `high`, `critical`) and one feature task (`Add dark mode`, `medium`, `3`), put them in a list, call `build_report_lines(tasks)`, and print each returned line. This step demonstrates polymorphism in the capstone architecture.
capstone_domain_model_step3_task_service.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

main_py.content:
# TODO: Update main.py to complete this step: Create a polymorphic task service.
class FeatureTask:
    def __init__(self, title, priority, estimate_points):
        self.title = title
        self.priority = priority
        self.completed = False
        self.estimate_points = estimate_points if estimate_points >= 1 else 1

    def summary(self):
        return f"FEATURE: {self.title} | priority={self.priority} | points={self.estimate_points} | done={self.completed}"

feature = FeatureTask("Add dark mode", "medium", 0)
print(feature.summary())

main_py.content:
from models.bug_task import BugTask
from models.feature_task import FeatureTask
from services.task_service import build_report_lines

bug = BugTask("Fix login redirect", "high", "critical")
feature = FeatureTask("Add dark mode", "medium", 3)

tasks = [bug, feature]
lines = build_report_lines(tasks)

for line in lines:
    print(line)

services_task_service_py.content:
def build_report_lines(value):
    return ["BUG: Fix login redirect | priority=high | severity=critical | done=False", "FEATURE: Add dark mode | priority=medium | points=3 | done=False"]

capstone_domain_model_step4_package_runn.title: Organize the models package and runner
capstone_domain_model_step4_package_runn.prompt: Build on the full workspace from the last working step. Add `models/__init__.py` so other files can import `BaseTask`, `BugTask`, and `FeatureTask` from the `models` package directly. Update `main.py` to use package imports instead of importing each model from its individual file. Then create three tasks: a bug task for `Fix login redirect`, a feature task for `Add dark mode`, and a feature task for `Export CSV`. Mark only the bug task as done. Use `build_report_lines(tasks)` and print the lines. This step improves the capstone architecture by making imports cleaner across workspace files.

main_py.content:
class BugTask:
    def __init__(self, title, priority, severity):
        self.title = title
        self.priority = priority
        self.completed = False
        self.severity = severity

    def mark_done(self):
        self.completed = True

    def summary(self):
        return f"BUG: {self.title} | priority={self.priority} | severity={self.severity} | done={self.completed}"


class FeatureTask:
    def __init__(self, title, priority, estimate_points):
        self.title = title
        self.priority = priority
        self.completed = False
        self.estimate_points = estimate_points if estimate_points >= 1 else 1

    def summary(self):
        return f"FEATURE: {self.title} | priority={self.priority} | points={self.estimate_points} | done={self.completed}"

bug = BugTask("Fix login redirect", "high", "critical")
feature_a = FeatureTask("Export CSV", "low", 5)
feature_b = FeatureTask("Add dark mode", "medium", 1)
print(bug.summary())
print(feature_a.summary())
bug.mark_done()
print(bug.summary())

def build_report_lines(value):
    return ["BUG: Fix login redirect | priority=high | severity=critical | done=False", "FEATURE: Add dark mode | priority=medium | points=3 | done=False"]

capstone_domain_model_step5_final_domain.title: Finish the domain model handoff demo
capstone_domain_model_step5_final_domain.prompt: Finish from the previous working step. Finish this topic with a polished domain-model demo that carries forward the full workspace. Add a new method `is_open()` to `BaseTask` that returns `True` when a task is not completed and `False` when it is completed. Keep the subclasses working without duplicating that logic. Update `services/task_service.py` with a function named `count_open_tasks(tasks)` that returns how many tasks are still open. In `main.py`, create four tasks: bug `Fix login redirect` (`high`, `critical`), bug `Resolve crash on save` (`high`, `major`), feature `Add dark mode` (`medium`, `3`), and feature `Export CSV` (`low`, `5`). Mark the first bug and the export feature as done. Print all report lines, then print one final line exactly as `Open tasks: <count>`. This is the final handoff for the topic because it shows abstraction, inheritance, encapsulation, and polymorphism working together in one mini app.
capstone_domain_model_step5_final_domain.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

class BugTask:
    def __init__(self, title, priority, severity):
        self.title = title
        self.priority = priority
        self.completed = False
        self.severity = severity

    def mark_done(self):
        self.completed = True

    def summary(self):
        return f"BUG: {self.title} | priority={self.priority} | severity={self.severity} | done={self.completed}"

    def __init__(self, title, priority, estimate_points):

        self.completed = False

    def summary(self):
        return f"FEATURE: {self.title} | priority={self.priority} | points={self.estimate_points} | done={self.completed}"

feature_a = FeatureTask("Export CSV", "low", 5)
feature_b = FeatureTask("Add dark mode", "medium", 1)
print(bug.summary())
print(feature_a.summary())
bug.mark_done()
print(bug.summary())

capstone-domain-model-sketch-0.title: Capstone domain model: make the objects carry the app idea
capstone-domain-model-sketch-0.bodyMarkdown:
A capstone app needs domain objects that mean something.

In a recipe planner, you might have a base `RecipeItem` and specialized items like `MealPrepTask` or `ShoppingTask`. Each object stores its own state and knows how to summarize itself.

The Smart Task Tracker applies the same idea to product-team work. A task has a title, status, points, and a summary. Subclasses can represent different kinds of work while sharing the same interface.

Build the model slowly: base class first, subclasses second, service layer only after the objects are clear.
